coredata.dc:title: Broad band (UV–VIS) photoluminescence from carbonized fumed silica: Emission, excitation and kinetic properties
coredata.dc:creator: Vasin, A.V.
Adlung, M.
Tertykh, V.A.
Kysil, D.
Gallis, S.
Nazarov, A.N.
Lysenko, V.S.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon incorporated silica nano-powder (SiO2:C) has been fabricated by chemical treatment of a fumed silica with tetramethoxysilane and subsequent annealing at 700°C in pure nitrogen ambient. Upon thermal treatment SiO2:C powder exhibits strong photoluminescence (PL) that spans in the range from near-ultraviolet to visible. Two characteristic broad bands, with their maxima at 2.5eV (Green-band) and 3.3eV (Violet-band), contribute to the observed broadband emission spectrum from SiO2:C. PL emission and PL excitation (PLE) studies in the temperature range of 10–300K revealed a different PL behavior pertaining to the change of the peak position of the green and violet bands with respect to temperature. The low-energy emission band showed a redshift in the peak position with decreasing temperature, in contrast to the blue-shift behavior of the violet-band. The latter PL behavior suggests a different origin for the observed emission bands. Complementary time-resolved PL analysis for both emission bands was carried out and yielded PL transients of a few nanoseconds. On the basis of PL/PLE studies and structural characterization by FTIR it is suggested that the origin of the broad band emission may be attributed to radiative recombination in carbon-related surface sites/clusters.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Typical HRTEM image of the morphology of pristine fumed silica.
(a) – Weight loss of pristine fumed silica (curve 1) and methoxysilica (curve 2); (b) – corresponding derivative curves.

(a) – FTIR spectra of pristine fumed silica precursor (spectrum 1), methoxysilica (spectrum 2), annealed methoxysilica (spectrum 3); (b) - magnified fragments of spectra of pristine fumed silica and annealed methoxysilica (SiO2:C) in the range of O-H absorption (inset – subtraction of the spectra).
Photoimage of SiO2:C powder in day light and under UV radiation (409nm LED).

PL spectra of annealed methoxysilica (SiO2:C) under 270nm (1), 360nm (2), 420nm (3), 480nm (4), 545nm (5), and PL spectra of pristine fumed silica excited by 250nm (6) and 375nm (7). Spectra of pristine silica are magnified by factor of 3.
Normalized emission and excitation PL spectra of SiO2:C at room temperature and at 10K.
PL peak position (a) and integrated intensity of PL band (b) as function of temperature.
(a) – Decay curve of the emission at 390nm (excitation by 255nm); (b) – decay curves of the emission at 500nm and 640nm (excitation by 405nm).
PL spectra of SiO2:C under 360nm excitation at room temperature (spectrum 1) and at 10K (spectrum 2). Curve 3 is a result of subtraction of spectrum 1 from spectrum 2.
UV–VIS absorption spectra of clean quartz plate (1), quartz plate covered by pristine fumed silica layer (2) and by methoxysilica layer (3).
Light emission spectrum of SiO2:C powder excited by 360nm (1) and emission spectrum of black body at 6000K (2). Inset illustrates position of emission spectrum in chromaticity diagram.

head.ce:title: Broad band (UV–VIS) photoluminescence from carbonized fumed silica: Emission, excitation and kinetic properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Lashkaryov Institute of Semiconductor Physics, Kyiv, Ukraine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chuiko Institute of Surface Chemistry, Kyiv, Ukraine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Technical University of Ukraine “KPI”, Kyiv, Ukraine

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Lashkaryov Institute of Semiconductor Physics, Kyiv, Ukraine.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon incorporated silica nano-powder (SiO2:C) has been fabricated by chemical treatment of a fumed silica with tetramethoxysilane and subsequent annealing at 700°C in pure nitrogen ambient. Upon thermal treatment SiO2:C powder exhibits strong photoluminescence (PL) that spans in the range from near-ultraviolet to visible. Two characteristic broad bands, with their maxima at 2.5eV (Green-band) and 3.3eV (Violet-band), contribute to the observed broadband emission spectrum from SiO2:C. PL emission and PL excitation (PLE) studies in the temperature range of 10–300K revealed a different PL behavior pertaining to the change of the peak position of the green and violet bands with respect to temperature. The low-energy emission band showed a redshift in the peak position with decreasing temperature, in contrast to the blue-shift behavior of the violet-band. The latter PL behavior suggests a different origin for the observed emission bands. Complementary time-resolved PL analysis for both emission bands was carried out and yielded PL transients of a few nanoseconds. On the basis of PL/PLE studies and structural characterization by FTIR it is suggested that the origin of the broad band emission may be attributed to radiative recombination in carbon-related surface sites/clusters.

ce:section.ce:para: Several different methods of synthesizing efficient light-emitting materials based on silicon oxycarbide (SiCxOy) have been recently reported; for example, by ion implantation of C ions in SiO2 thin layer [1–4] and sol–gel processing [5,6], by deposition of thin films using the techniques of chemical vapor deposition [7,8] and magnetron sputtering [9], by oxidation of amorphous carbon-rich a-SiCy:H thin films [10] and carbonized porous silicon (por-Si:C) layers [11–13], and by carbonization of nanostructured silica powders [14,15] and silica nanowires [16]. One of the common features of light emission in such materials is the broad PL emission band that covers the spectral range from near ultraviolet (UV) to near infrared.

Nevertheless, due to the diversity of the processes employed in the synthesis of SiCxOy-based materials, there has been no general accepted concept on the origin of the observed luminescence emission. Furthermore, the different PL experimental conditions employed by the different research groups pose certain difficulties in adequately comparing the PL data, as the spectral distribution of the broad PL band is strongly dependent on the excitation photon energy due its inhomogeneous broadening.

Among the recent proposed origins of the visible light emission from such materials are intrinsic point defects in SiO2 matrix [16,17] and C-related point defects and/or Si–O–C local bonds [7]. Additionally, it has been demonstrated that carbon incorporation plays a key role in the formation of light emission centers, but structural identification of such centers still remains a challenge. In [9], a red shift in the low-energy part of the PL band with the increase of carbon incorporation in silicon oxide films was observed, and it was suggested that the PL may originate from carbon clusters. Similarly, a red spectral shift with increasing annealing temperature was also observed in SiO2:C nano-powder [14] and porous SiO2:C layers [13] and such behavior was attributed to an increase of the size of carbon clusters.

For the development of SiCxOy-based light-emitting material for practical applications in optoelectronics and in order to further our understanding on the origin of the visible light emission from carbon incorporated nanostructured silicas (SiO2:C), more detailed PL studies are required. To this end in the present report, the PL properties of carbon incorporated silica (SiO2:C) nano-powder will be presented under UV/visible excitation at temperatures in the range of 10–300K. The latter will be experimentally approached by PL and PL excitation (PLE) analyses in conjunction with time-resolved and temperature dependence PL measurements.

Fumed silica with a specific surface area of 295m2/g was used as a precursor for the synthesis of SiO2:C nano-powder. Pristine silica was preliminarily heated at 400°C for 2h in air to remove any adsorbed contaminations and was subsequently cooled avoiding contact with atmospheric moisture. Subsequent liquid-phase modification was performed in toluene solution of tetramethoxysilane (Si(OCH3)4) via the reaction of substitution in surface silanol groups.
≡Si-OH + CH3-O-Si(OCH3)3 → ≡Si-O-Si(OCH3)2,3 + CH3OH

After washing and drying, the chemically modified silica (methoxysilica) was annealed at 700°C in pure nitrogen flow at atmospheric pressure for 30min resulting in the desorption/pyrolytic decomposition of surface organosilicon groups.

Thermally activated desorption in pristine fumed silica and methoxysilica was characterized by thermogravimetry at atmospheric conditions with the use of a derivatograph Q-1500D apparatus (Paulik & Erdey). Local interatomic bonding was characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) in transmission mode (Nicolet 6700/Nicolet Continuum, Thermo Fisher Scientific). PL emission and excitation (PLE) measurements were performed in the temperature range of 10–300K using a Fluorolog III system (FL3-22, Horiba Jobin–Yvon). Optical absorption properties in UV–VIS spectral range were examined by SPECORD-40M double beam spectrophotometer. The PL spectra were corrected for the photomultiplier sensitivity and for the intensity of the excitation source (Xe lamp). Room-temperature PL decay transients were carried out in a LIFESPEC II spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments) with the use of two types of semiconductor laser as excitation source: 255nm excitation (pulse duration of 900ps; pulse repetition of 500ns; radiation power 5 μW) and 405nm excitation (pulse duration 50ps; pulse repetition 500ns; radiation power 5mW).

Morphology of the fumed silica (specific surface area of 295m2/g) is represented typically by spherical particle with diameter of about 10nm and their aggregates and agglomerates ( Fig. 1). Surface of the particles is terminated by HO groups that are partially replaced by organosilicon groups (-Si-(OCH3)n) after chemical modification by tetramethoxysilane (hereafter - methoxysilica).

The evolution of weight loss of pristine silica and methoxysilica as a function of temperature in the range of 50–900°C and the corresponding derivative curves are, respectively, shown in Fig. 2a and b. Both materials exhibit strong weight loss at the temperature as low as 100°C due to water desorption (Fig. 2a). Water desorption in methoxysilica is significantly weaker than in pristine silica because large part of surface hydroxyl groups is now replaced by organosilicon groups. Significant weight loss, which is associated with desorption of volatile hydrocarbons, is observed in methoxysilica at 270 and 320°C (Fig. 2b). It is worth noting that the derivative curve of methoxysilica exhibits a broad tail of weight loss at 400–600°C. Taking into account that experiment was performed at air ambient, this tail can be attributed to the weight loss due to oxidation of pyrolytic carbon by atmospheric oxygen.

A typical IR transmission spectrum of pristine fumed silica precursor is shown in Fig. 3a (spectrum 1). The silicon oxide matrix is represented by the absorption band at 1000–1200cm−1 (symmetrical stretching Si–O–Si) with a low-frequency shoulder at 970cm−1 (stretching Si–OH), and a weaker band ~800cm−1 (asymmetrical stretching Si–O–Si) [18–20]. The 1960cm−1 and 1870cm−1 bands correspond to the overtone/combination of vibration modes inherent to nanosilicas with high specific surface area [21]. The broad absorption band in the range of 3000–3800cm−1 and the narrow band at 1630cm−1 correspond, respectfully, to stretching and bending vibration modes of O–H [21].

Upon chemical modification well-defined IR bands appeared at 2800–3000cm−1 and 1474cm−1 that are attributed to the stretching and bending vibration modes of C(sp
3 )–Hn bonds [22]. The apparent peak ~1705cm−1 is associated with carbonyl groups C˭O [22]. The presence of Si–O–C bonding, presumably available on the surface of methoxysilica, cannot be verified as its corresponding absorption is in the range of 1000–1200cm−1 [22,23], where there is a strong absorption in the host silica matrix.

The C–H related as well as the C˭O bands disappeared upon annealing (Fig. 3a, spectrum 3). The comparison of the normalized spectra between the pristine fumed silica precursor and the annealed methoxysilica in the spectral range of O–H bending vibrations (in Fig. 3b) allows to find a weak but well defined increase of the absorption on the low-frequency shoulder of O–H related band. The subtraction of their spectra reveals a peak at approximately 1608cm−1, which can be attributed to the stretching vibration of C(sp2)=C(sp2) bonds [22], indicating sp2-carbon precipitation in the annealed methoxysilica upon annealing. Carbon C(sp2)=C(sp2) bonds typically are not polar and, therefore, are weakly active in IR absorption. Hence, even small traces of characteristic IR absorption indicate significant concentration of corresponding bonds. Presumably existing C(sp3)–C(sp3) single bonds cannot be detected in the IR spectrum because their corresponding absorption (natively also weak) is expected to be near 1100cm−1. Furthermore, our attempts to identify pyrolytical carbon by Raman scattering using laser excitations at either 488nm or 514nm were not successful due to the strong background of the sample's PL as excited by the laser probe.

Under ultraviolet excitation, the pristine fumed silica showed featureless weak PL band in broad spectral range covering near-UV and visible spectral range with maximum intensity around 450nm and near-UV shoulder. PL in the pure silica nanoparticles is commonly attributed to the surface states associated with metastable structural defects [24,25]. Upon chemical modification the intensity of the PL emission of the powder did not increase noticeably whereas the annealed methoxysilica showed strong white luminescence emission, which is well seen in bright room conditions using semiconductor pen-laser as the excitation source (see Fig. 4). It is important to note that the chemically untreated powder did not exhibit any noticeable enhancement of light emission after the annealing treatment at the same conditions indicating crucial role of surface modification.

PL spectra excited by radiation in range of 270–545nm are illustrated in Fig. 5. Under 270nm excitation room temperature emission spectra are composed by two primary PL bands (Fig. 5, spectrum 1) with theirs peak positions at approximately 380nm/3.2eV and 500nm/2.5eV. A single broad featureless PL band is observed under excitation 360nm and larger wavelength. PL band is narrowing and gradually redshifted as excitation wavelength is increased up to 545nm. Spectra 6 and 7 in Fig. 5 illustrate magnified PL spectra of pristine silica excited by 250nm and 375nm. It is seen that PL band of pristine silica does not exhibit excitation induced red shift of PL band in contrast to annealed methoxysilica.

To obtain more insight into the nature of the PL emission bands the PL emission/excitation properties were examined in three spectral ranges: high-energy (near-UV/violet; V-band), medium energy (green; G-band) and low-energy (orange; O-band). Fig. 6 illustrates PL emission and excitation at room temperature and at 10K. As it can be determined from Fig. 6a, there is no appreciable temperature effect on the relative contribution of two components of PL band; a minor spectral change was only observed. In particular, the low-energy band red-shifts as the temperature decreases from 300K to 10K, while the high energy band exhibits a minor blue-shift at 10K.

The PL excitation (PLE) curve of the violet emission (415nm/3eV) is presented in Fig. 6a. At room temperature the intensity of the 415nm emission increases monotonically with increasing excitation energy up to 250nm/5.0eV (Fig. 6a), whereas low-temperature PLE exhibits a maximum at 285nm/4.35eV.

The room-temperature emission spectrum excited at 360nm/3.44eV exhibits a single broad band with the maximum around 490nm/2.55eV, which slightly red-shifts to 500nm/2.48eV at 10K (Fig. 6b). As far as its PLE behavior of the green emission (500nm) also increases monotonically with increasing excitation energy, but presents two peaks at 300nm/4.13eV and 360nm/3.4eV.

Regarding the orange-red PL emission spectra under 545nm/2.28eV excitation has a maximum at 610nm/2.0eV. The PLE curve of the emission peak exhibits a pronounced excitation band with maximum at about 480–500nm/2.53eV at both low temperature as well as at room temperature (Fig. 6c). UV part of PLE curve demonstrates growth at room temperature while at 10K there is no obvious PL enhancement with increase of photon energy and PLE peak at about 320nm can be now observed.

The temperature dependence of spectral position of the aforementioned three PL peaks is illustrated in Fig. 7a. The near-UV emission (V-band) peaks demonstrated a small energy redshift for temperatures above 120K, whereas the green band (G-band) emission peak blue-shifted. No shift of the orange-red emission peak (O-band) was observed in the investigated temperature range.

Fig. 7b illustrates the evolution of the integrated intensity of PL as a function of temperature IL(T), under 270nm, 360nm and 545nm excitation. Under 270nm excitation, IPL(T) decreased by 30% with increasing temperature, up to 200K, followed by a small increase at higher temperatures. The effect of temperature on PL intensity under 360nm and 545nm excitation seems quite similar, but the quenching in the temperature range 10–200K is less significant.

The PL decay transient behavior of the V- and G-/O-bands in SiO2:C was studied using, respectively, 255nm and 405nm excitation and illustrated in Fig. 8. PL decay transients do not follow a single exponential law and rather be approximated by stretched exponent.
I ( t )
=
I
0
exp
−
(
t
τ
)
β
where τ is the characteristic time and the parameters 0<β<1 characterize the deviation from the single exponential. It is commonly accepted that in disordered systems with broad distribution of excited states lifetimes, which describes the elementary relaxation radiative or nonradiative processes, the PL decay can be described by a stretched exponential law. Such decay behavior can be interpreted as a linear superposition of an ensemble of simple exponential decays and has been observed in different disordered materials including pure silica nanoparticles [26]. Decay dynamics in SiO2:C were well fitted by this expression with the fitting parameters indicated in Fig. 8, which may suggest that the broad PL may also originate from broad distribution of structural disorder in emission centers.

Let consider temperature behavior of PL peaks (Fig. 7b). Due to general consideration the temperature-induced shifts of PL can be caused by following effects: (1) change of the energy of electron states due to thermal expansion effect and electron-phonon interaction; (2) temperature dependent evolution of excitation/recombination paths of elementary excitations in highly disordered system with broad energy and spatial distribution of electron states.

For one, the red-shift of the V-band emission with increasing temperature could be attributed to a decrease of the transition energy in the corresponding emission centers due to electron-phonon interaction [27]. Secondly, the blue-shift of the G-band peak cannot be caused by a temperature-induced change of the energy of electron states, and therefore, may be instead associated with temperature-dependent redistribution of excitation/recombination paths.

Fig. 9 represents not normalized PL spectra of SiO2:C under 360nm excitation at room temperature (spectrum 1) and at 10K (spectrum 2). It is well seen that maximum intensity of PL spectrum at low temperature is redshifted. Comparing two spectra one can note that spectral shift is due to evolution of spectral distribution in low-energy spectral range. Subtracting spectrum 1 from spectrum 2 results in broad spectral distribution peaked at about 560–570nm (curve 3). Now it can be concluded that spectral shift of G-band peak is associated with spectral dispersion of temperature dependent emission efficiency in the low energy tail of PL band. Indeed, such interpretation is in well agreement with temperature dependent evolution of integrated intensity of G- and O-bands (Fig. 7b): intensity of G-band at room temperature is reduced for about 10% in comparison with low temperature spectrum while O-band reduced for about 20%.

Temperature behavior of the excitation features of the emission in different spectral ranges is also worth a detailed discussion. The excitation spectrum of the PL within V-band does not exhibit distinct excitation peaks at room temperature, whereas the pronounced band at 250nm is observed at low temperature (Fig. 6a). Such difference can be interpreted as an evidence of energy coupling of surface emission centers and absorption centers in silica matrix at room temperature. Thermally assisted energy transfer is diminished at low temperature and excitation features relating namely to emission centers become apparent. Similar behavior demonstrates excitation curve of green emission (G-band) but excitation band at low temperature is now redshifted to 300nm (Fig. 6b) with obvious shoulder at 370nm.

In contrast, the orange-red emission (O-band) demonstrates a pronounced excitation band in visible range at low temperature as well as at 10K (Fig. 6c). The latter may suggest that absorption of excitation photons with low-energy in silica host is weak, and therefore, absorption and emission occurs directly at the luminescent centers without mediation of the silica-related states regardless the temperature. Weak UV excitation band at 320nm can be identified at low temperature while there is no signs of PLE peak at room temperature indicating that this excitation band is mediated by absorption in silica at room temperature.

Usually photoluminescence of pure amorphous nanosilicas is considered in terms of optically active structural defects, both bulk and surface. It is well known that photon emission of bulk defects in near-UV and visible spectral range is associated with radiative triplet-singlet transition (T1→S0) i.e. “phosphorescence” [28], which is natively slow process with characteristic decay time of micro- and milliseconds, or even seconds [29]. In our case the characteristic PL decay time is of several nanoseconds, therefore light emission cannot be attributed to bulk silica defect.

It have been reported that surface-related visible photoluminescence in silica nanoparticles is characterized by nanosecond decay [24,26,30,31]. To explain the PL transient, a structural model of emissive surface defects (e.g. dioxisilirane and sililene pair defect) has been proposed [24,26,31]. However, it is important to note that dioxisilirane and sililene functional groups are unstable and at high temperature, such defects are expected to be transformed into more stable but not emissive surface bonding termination. Indeed, the authors reported in [26] that they observed no surface-related PL from dioxisilirane and sililene after annealing of nanosilica at temperature above 600°C. Therefore, these defects may not be likely responsible for the strong PL observed in our sample upon annealing at temperature as high as 700°C. This conclusion is consistent with our previous studies of nanoporous SiO2:C materials that exhibited strong PL after thermal treatment at temperature as high as 800°C [11,12].

Nanoseconds decay time of broad PL bands in fumed silica nanoparticles was observed also by Vaccaro et. al. after annealing at 750°C [30]. The authors assigned the 2.27eV band to silanone surface groups (Si˭O). But it well known that silanone functional groups are extremely unstable due to high reactivity. Hence, the presence of silanone groups after high temperature treatment is quite questionable. It is also worth noting that sample preparation procedure in this report involved incorporation of carbon in form of methoxy-groups (-OCH3), so that carbon-related surface species cannot be excluded from consideration in their study.

As it was mentioned above, chemically untreated silica was annealed at the same conditions as the carbon-incorporated samples but PL intensity in these samples was more than one order of magnitude weaker PL compared to its carbon-incorporated counterpart. Therefore, the role of carbon incorporation can be deductively highlighted.

At temperature higher then pyrolytical decomposition threshold volatile organic components removed from the material but some of nonvolatile pyrolytical carbon is obviously remains in condensed state. Carbon is insoluble in silicon oxide at room temperature, so that there is no thermodynamically stable solid state alloy of carbon and silica at room temperature. This means that the distribution of carbon in form of single atom interstitial/substitution in bulk SiO2 network is unfavorable, so that the most possible structural form of carbon incorporation into highly dispersed SiO2 powder is surface clusters and/or nanoparticles.

In recent years it was widely reported that carbon nanodots (CNDs), including graphene nanodots (GNDs) and graphene oxide nanodots (GONDs) exhibit broad band photoluminescence in near-UV/visible spectral range [32–35]. Light emission from CNDs with purely sp2-coordinated structure is believed to be associated with the presence of highest energy occupied molecular orbital /lowest energy unoccupied orbital (HOMO-LUMO) energy gap, by analogy with poliaromatic hydrocarbon molecules [36] or graphite nanoparticles [37]. This gap should obviously be dependent on the of the cluster. Nevertheless, this is different in structural model of GONDs that have a high fraction of sp3-coordinated carbon atoms and high concentration of C–O bonding that introduce additional localized electron states [38]. Electronic transitions in such cluster involve not only bonding and antibonding orbitals of carbon atoms (σ–σ*, π–π*) but nonbonding orbitals (n) of oxygen atoms as well (n–σ* and n–π*). Among these transitions, π–π* and n–π* transitions are of particular concern because their recombination transition energies are supposed to be responsible for visible light emission in CNDs. It was demonstrated that PL peak of GONDs can be tuned from near-UV (340nm) to visible (420nm) spectral range by degree of reduction [39]. Later it was shown that PL band in GONDs is definitely multicomponent and composed by at list two bands (commonly blue and green) that sometimes identified as “excitation-independent” and “excitation-dependent” [40,41] because spectral position of high-energy band does not effected by excitation wavelength while low-energy band is widely reported as strongly dependent on excitation photon energy quite similar to G-band (Fig. 4). PL decay in CNDs/GNDs/GONDs structures is ranging from hundreds of picoseconds to several nanoseconds i.e. quite similar to that observed in SiO2:C.

The presence of carbon precipitates in SiO2:C was further investigated by means of UV–VIS absorption. It is worth noting that absorption measurements of SiO2:C powder in transmission mode can be quite problematic due to particles agglomeration during the thermal treatment, which cannot readily and homogeneously dissolve in liquid solvent (water or alcohol). UV–VIS absorption measurements of SiO2:C was carried out by the following procedure. SiO2:C powder was mechanically grinded followed by dispersion in alcohol. Such suspension is not transparent due to high scattering from microparticles; hence a drop of suspension was placed on one side of a quartz plate and spread out carefully over its surface. After drying a thin transparent layer of SiO2:C was formed on the surface of the quartz plate. Fig. 10 shows typical absorption spectra of the SiO2:C powder and the quartz substrate. A pronounced absorption band with a maximum ~260nm was observed, which strongly resembles the band seen in carbon nanodots and can be assigned to π−π* transitions in C˭C [42,43]. Absorption due to n−π* states in oxygen atoms is usually observed in CNDs ~300nm [43]. Traces of such absorption can be identified in the absorption spectrum of SiO2:C as marked by the arrow in Fig. 10.

Based on our PL/PLE data and in conjunction with the structural characterization by FTIR studies the broad multicomponent PL band in SiO2:C nanopowder may therefore be assigned to surface carbon nanoprecipitates by analogy with CNDs/GNDs/GONDs. In this case the two different PL bands can be attributed to different kinds of precipitates. In frame of “carbon-hypothesis” it can be explained as following. The high energy V-band is suggested to originate from carbon clusters with structure similar to GOND, i.e. with high fraction of carbon atoms in sp3-state and bonded to silica surface. Pertaining to the G-band this may be attributed to “graphene-like” amorphous carbon clusters with a dominant sp2-coordination of atoms and a weaker bonding to silica surface. To this end the low-energy tail of G-band (i.e. the O-band) may be assigned to larger sp2-clusters with weak or even no bonding to surface (the larger cluster the smaller emission energy and efficiency). Formation of amorphous carbon clusters in SiO2:C powder is indirectly evidenced by change of the color of the powder from white to light-brown after annealing and appearance of strong EPR signal with g-factor about 2.0030 that is close to typical values in disordered carbonaceous materials including fumed silica carbonized using pyrolysis of phenyl groups [14].

Due to very broad spectral distribution of the PL in visible range the emission of SiO2:C powder under UV excitation looks as white light. Representative PL spectrum under excitation by 360nm and emission spectrum of black body at 6000K within visible range is illustrated in Fig. 11 for comparison. Emission of black-body at 6000K can be considered as rough simulation of solar radiation i.e. natural day-lighting. One can see that spectral distribution of PL is almost identical to that of black body emission in visible range. Analyzing of spectral properties of PL band in terms of RGB concept (in frame of Chromaticity diagram) results in correlated color temperature 5940K and color rendering index 97 that is extremely promising parameters as inexpensive and environment friendly single component phosphor for artificial white light sources like white LEDs with near-UV excitation or compact fluorescent lamps. Preliminary evaluation showed the external quantum yield (QY) is in range 10–20% and needs further development towards commercially competitive values.

Carbon incorporated silica SiO2:C nano-powder was synthesized by successive chemical treatment of fumed silica in tetramethoxysilane followed by annealing at 700°C in pure nitrogen. Two distinct emission bands were found in photoluminescence spectra and attributed to different emission centers. It is suggested that the PL properties of the SiO2:C nano-powder can be explained by self-consistent carbon-based model according to which light emission originates from carbon precipitates (nanodots) dispersed on silica surface.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support from Ministry of Education and Science of Ukraine (Project F2904).

Color control of white photoluminescence from carbon-incorporated silicon oxide
